perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time with 30 days written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time with 30 days written notice, unless otherwise prohibited by law.
perturbation.explanation: This modification adds the phrase "unless otherwise prohibited by law" which contradicts the absolute right to terminate the agreement with only a 30 day notice. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in Florida contracts, which prevents a party from terminating a contract to deprive the other party of its expected benefits, especially if the other party has substantially performed its obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, preventing parties from acting in a way that would unfairly undermine the other party's benefits, and the added clause would weaken the covenant, granting excessive power to arbitrarily end the agreement, especially if UTK invested considerable resources into technology identification.

F.S. 671.304 671.304 Laws not repealed; precedence where code provisions in conflict with other laws; certain statutory remedies retained. — (1) The article on documents of title (Art. 7) does not repeal or modify any laws prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees’ businesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein; but the fact that such laws are violated does not affect the status of a document of title which otherwise complies with the definition of a “document of title” (s. 671.201 ). (2) The following laws and parts of laws are specifically not repealed and shall take precedence over any provisions of this code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 517 — Sale of securities. (b) Chapter 687 — Interest and usury; lending practices. (c) Chapter 516 — Florida Consumer Finance Act. (d) Chapter 520 — Retail installment sales (Part I, Motor Vehicle Sales Finance Act; Part III, Retail Installment Sales Act; Part IV, Installment Sales Finance Act). (e) Chapter 665 — Florida Savings Association Act. (f) Chapter 657 — Credit unions. (g) Chapters 319 and 328 — Title certificates (motor vehicle; vessel). (h) Chapter 538 — Secondhand Dealers and Secondary Metals Recyclers. (i) Chapters 658, 660, and 663. (3) The following laws or parts of laws, although not repealed, shall yield to and be superseded by any provisions of the code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 697 — Instruments deemed mortgages and the nature of a mortgage. (b) Chapter 701 — Assignment and cancellation of mortgages. (c) Chapter 702 — Foreclosure of mortgages. (d) Chapter 727 — General assignments. (4) Notwithstanding any provisions to the contrary in any of the following Florida Statutes, the remedies provided by such statutes shall not restrict the remedies otherwise available to a secured party under this code, but all such remedies shall be cumulatively
perturbation.justification: The added phrase "unless otherwise prohibited by law" introduces a condition that did not previously exist, creating a loophole in the original agreement. This could weaken the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation, allowing a party to potentially terminate the agreement for reasons that would unfairly undermine the other party's benefits. While the provided legal snippet does not directly address the covenant of good faith, the Law Explanation suggests that this addition could be interpreted as an excessive grant of power to terminate the agreement arbitrarily, thereby contradicting the spirit of fair dealing implied in contracts. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, as it removes some of the consumer protection that the contract granted before modification, creating a loophole.

perturbation.original_text: A. UTK shall not divulge to others, any trade secret or confidential information, knowledge, or data concerning or pertaining to the business and affairs of AVDU, obtained by UTK as a result of its engagement hereunder, unless authorized, in writing by AVDU. UTK represents and warrants that it has established appropriate internal procedures for protecting the trade secrets and confidential information of AVDU, including, without limitation, restrictions on disclosure of such information to employees and other persons who may be engaged in such information to employees and other persons who may be engaged in rendering services to any person, firm or entity which may be a competitor of AVDU.
perturbation.changed_text: UTK shall not divulge to others any trade secret or confidential information, knowledge, or data concerning or pertaining to the business and affairs of AVDU, obtained by UTK as a result of its engagement hereunder, unless authorized in writing by AVDU.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause concerning internal procedures for protecting trade secrets, the agreement fails to explicitly address the measures UTK will take to safeguard AVDU's confidential information. This omission creates potential legal risk in Florida, as Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets to qualify for protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 688.002 outlines injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. A key element for trade secret protection under FUTSA is demonstrating that the trade secret holder took reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of the information. By omitting UTK's representation about established internal procedures, the contract weakens AVDU's ability to claim trade secret misappropriation in case of a breach, as it's less clear that reasonable measures were agreed upon and implemented.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The modification omits UTK's representation regarding established internal procedures for protecting AVDU's confidential information. This creates a legal risk because, under Florida Statute § 688.002(4)(b), a trade secret must be 'the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' The original text explicitly stated UTK had 'established appropriate internal procedures,' demonstrating reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By removing this clause, the changed text weakens AVDU's ability to claim trade secret misappropriation, as it reduces evidence of agreed-upon measures to protect the trade secret, thus contradicting the requirement for reasonable efforts.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration for providing these Services, AVDU shall pay UTK $120,000 worth of unregistered shares of common stock (31,413 shares) upon the execution of this Strategic Alliance Agreement. 1/12th of the shares (2,617) shall vest each month during the term of this Agreement. If this Agreement is terminated any unvested shares will be returned to AVDU. Both AVDU and UTK will cover their own out-of-pocket expenses incurred during the performance of this Strategic Alliance Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration for providing these Services, AVDU shall pay UTK $120,000 worth of unregistered shares of common stock (31,413 shares) upon the execution of this Strategic Alliance Agreement. Both AVDU and UTK will cover their own out-of-pocket expenses incurred during the performance of this Strategic Alliance Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause concerning the vesting schedule and the return of unvested shares upon termination, the agreement lacks clarity on how the equity compensation is earned and what happens if the agreement ends prematurely.  This omission can lead to disputes and potential violations of Florida's wage payment laws if UTK is considered an employee or if the shares are deemed wages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes Chapter 532: Payment of Wages
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 532 addresses the payment of wages.  While the contract designates UTK as an independent contractor, the lack of a clear vesting schedule and return policy for the shares could create ambiguity. If the stock compensation is construed as 'wages' under Florida law, the omission would run afoul of the requirements for timely payment and procedures upon termination. Without the vesting schedule, UTK could argue for full compensation even upon early termination, potentially violating AVDU's rights under the statute if the compensation is deemed unearned under a reasonable interpretation.

perturbation.justification: The original text included a vesting schedule for the shares and a provision for returning unvested shares upon termination. The changed text omits these provisions. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that this omission creates ambiguity regarding how the equity is earned and what happens upon early termination, potentially conflicting with Florida wage payment laws if the stock is considered wages. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that without the vesting schedule, UTK could argue for full compensation even upon early termination, potentially violating AVDU's rights if the compensation is deemed unearned. Scraped Law Snippet 1, by pointing to Title XXXI regarding labor laws, indirectly supports the argument that Florida wage laws may be applicable if the stock compensation is considered wages, making the omission a potential legal issue. The definition of Omission Legal Contradiction states that it happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.
